questions.questionText: ASA Physical Status Classification — a patient with severe systemic disease that is a constant threat to life is classified as:
questions.optionB: ASA IV (incapacitating systemic disease that is a constant threat to life)
questions.explanation: Core concept: ASA classification predicts perioperative risk. Why correct: ASA I: healthy. ASA II: mild systemic disease (controlled HTN, mild DM, obesity BMI 30-40, smoker, social drinker). ASA III: severe systemic disease (uncontrolled DM, COPD with limitations, morbid obesity, active hepatitis, moderate-severe CHF). ASA IV: severe disease that is CONSTANT THREAT TO LIFE (recent MI <3 months, ongoing cardiac ischemia, severe valve dysfunction, sepsis, DIC). ASA V: moribund, not expected to survive 24 hours without surgery. ASA VI: brain-dead organ donor. Why A wrong: ASA II = mild disease. Why C wrong: ASA III = severe but not constant threat. Why D wrong: ASA V = moribund. Clinical relevance: ASA correlates with perioperative mortality. Exam trap: 'Constant threat to life' is the key phrase for ASA IV. A 'severe systemic disease' alone is ASA III. Memory aid: 'ASA IV = Imminent threat (IV = I'm Very sick).' Reference: Aitkenhead – Preoperative Assessment.
questions.conceptTag: ASA classification IV constant threat perioperative

questions.questionText: The recommended nil-per-oral (NPO/fasting) time for clear fluids before elective surgery in adults is:
questions.optionB: 2 hours (clear fluids: water, apple juice, black tea/coffee — '2-4-6 rule': 2h clear fluids, 4h breast milk, 6h light meal/formula)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Modern fasting guidelines — 2 hours for clear fluids (not 'nil by mouth from midnight'). Why correct: ASA/ESA guidelines: clear liquids = 2 hours, breast milk = 4 hours, formula/light meal = 6 hours, heavy/fatty meal = 8 hours. Reduces dehydration, hypoglycemia, patient discomfort. Why A wrong: 8 hours only for heavy/fatty meals. Outdated 'nil from midnight' causes unnecessary dehydration. Why C wrong: 12 hours is excessive and harmful. Why D wrong: 1 hour is too short — aspiration risk. Clinical relevance: Purpose: prevent aspiration of gastric contents (Mendelson syndrome — pH <2.5, volume >25 mL). Full stomach patients: rapid sequence induction with cricoid pressure (Sellick maneuver). Exam trap: Clear fluids ≠ orange juice/milk (these are NOT clear — contain particles/fat). Clear = see-through (water, apple juice, black tea). Memory aid: '2-4-6-8 fasting rule: Clear 2, Breast 4, Light 6, Heavy 8.' Reference: Aitkenhead – Preoperative Fasting.
questions.conceptTag: NPO fasting 2-4-6 rule clear fluids aspiration

questions.questionText: A patient on anticoagulants requires surgery. Warfarin should be stopped how many days before elective surgery?
questions.optionB: 5 days before surgery (INR target <1.5 for neuraxial block; warfarin has long half-life ~36-42 hours, takes 4-5 days for INR normalization)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Warfarin half-life ~36-42h → stop 5 days before surgery for INR normalization. Why correct: Warfarin has long half-life (factor-dependent: factor VII 6h, factor X 36h, factor II 60h → full effect wears off in 4-5 days). Target INR <1.5 for safe surgery/neuraxial block. Why A wrong: 1 day insufficient — factors still depleted. Why C wrong: 12 hours — only applicable for DOACs (rivaroxaban/apixaban in low-risk surgery). Why D wrong: Dangerous — bleeding risk. Clinical relevance: Bridging with LMWH if high thromboembolic risk (mechanical valve, recent VTE). Check INR on morning of surgery. Reverse urgently with IV vitamin K + PCC (prothrombin complex concentrate) or FFP. Exam trap: DOACs (apixaban/rivaroxaban): stop 1-2 days before. Aspirin: usually continued for most surgeries (except neurosurgery). Memory aid: 'Warfarin = 5 Whole days to wear off (long half-life).' Reference: Aitkenhead – Perioperative Anticoagulation.
questions.conceptTag: Warfarin stop 5 days surgery INR bridging
